step_by_step_thinking:
The patient's death suggests there was significant myocardial injury leading up to his demise. Tetrazolium chloride staining is commonly used to identify areas of viable vs. non-viable myocardium following an infarction. Infarcted myocardium appears dark brown upon staining because the stain does not take up into necrotic cells but instead stains the surrounding normal tissues. Therefore, given that the patient experienced fatal chest pain indicative of a myocardial infarction prior to death, the stained myocardium would show dark brown regions corresponding to the area of infarction.
\boxed{C} Based on the information about tetrazolium chloride staining showing dark brown regions in infarcted myocardium, I conclude:
\boxed{C}
Given that the patient suffered a fatal myocardial infarction indicated by pre-death chest pain, the resulting myocardium would appear dark brown upon tetrazolium chloride staining. Thus,

Tetrazolium chloride staining is commonly used to identify areas of viable vs. non-viable myocardium following an infarction. Infarcted myocardium appears dark brown upon staining because the stain does not take up into necrotic cells but instead stains the surrounding normal tissues. Therefore, given that the patient experienced fatal chest pain indicative of a myocardial infarction prior to death, the stained myocardium would show dark brown regions corresponding to the area of infarction.
\boxed{C} Based on the information about tetrazolium chloride staining showing dark brown regions in infarcted myocardium, I conclude:

Given that the patient suffered a fatal myocardial infarction indicated by pre-death chest pain, the resulting myocardium would appear dark brown upon tetrazolium chloride staining. Thus, option C "Dark brown" accurately describes what the myocardium would look like post-autopsy.
